title: Apneic Infant in Waiting Room
hpi: 3-month-old male brought back from the waiting room by a parent after staff noticed he was limp and unresponsive. Mom reports he was seen in clinic 3 days ago for upper respiratory symptoms. In the waiting room he had an episode where he turned blue and stopped breathing for approximately 30 seconds, then took a few gasping breaths. He is now minimally responsive.

pmh: Born at 35 weeks gestation. No prior hospitalization. Up to date on vaccinations. No known medications.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 1: Requires immediate life-saving intervention. Apnea, SpO2 52%, GCS 8, agonal respirations — this patient requires immediate bag-mask ventilation and likely intubation. Delay of even one minute is unacceptable. Classic presentation of RSV bronchiolitis progressing to respiratory failure in a premature infant.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies agonal breathing, SpO2 52%, and GCS 8 as ESI 1 criteria requiring immediate resuscitative intervention.
teaching_point: In infants <6 months, especially ex-premature, RSV can progress rapidly to apnea and respiratory failure. Any SpO2 <90% with altered mental status in this age group is ESI 1 until proven otherwise.

chief_complaint: Allergic reaction after eating peanut butter cookie
hpi: 4-year-old female presents via EMS 12 minutes after accidental ingestion of a peanut butter cookie at a birthday party. Known peanut allergy. Father administered one dose of diphenhydramine 12.5mg en route. EMS reports audible stridor and diffuse urticaria noted at scene. Blood pressure was 68/38 on scene. She is anxious, crying, and complaining of throat tightness.

teaching_point: Diphenhydramine is NOT first-line for anaphylaxis and does not treat hemodynamic instability or upper airway edema. Epinephrine is the only effective immediate treatment. Antihistamines alone in the face of hemodynamic compromise represent a common dangerous error.

Does not meet ESI 1 currently (no immediate life-saving intervention needed this second) but cannot wait. The AI's failure here illustrates a critical limitation: it may process vitals and surface features without applying the syndromic pattern recognition that an experienced clinician uses.

title: Croup with Stridor at Rest
hpi: 3-year-old male with 2-day history of barky, seal-like cough and low-grade fever. Tonight developed audible inspiratory stridor at rest. Parents report he woke up from sleep with the noisy breathing. No drooling. No dysphagia. No recent choking events. No ill contacts with epiglottitis. Has not received nebulized racemic epinephrine previously.
exam: Alert, mildly anxious, sitting upright in parent's lap. Audible inspiratory stridor at rest (not only with crying). Mild intercostal retractions. Good air entry bilaterally. No drooling. Neck supple. No lymphadenopathy. Moderate barky cough during exam. Oropharynx normal without erythema, no exudates.
pmh: No prior croup episodes. No prior intubation or stridor. No hospitalizations. No medications. No known anatomic airway abnormalities.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 3: Stridor at rest represents moderate-severe croup (Westley score ≥3) and requires multiple resources — nebulized racemic epinephrine, dexamethasone (oral or IM), pulse oximetry monitoring, and extended observation period (minimum 2 hours post-epinephrine to assess for rebound). This is NOT a one-resource visit. Stridor at REST (vs only with agitation) is the critical distinction.
ai_rationale: AI INCORRECTLY assigns ESI 4. The AI identifies 'croup needing dexamethasone' as a single-resource visit, missing the clinical significance of stridor at rest vs only with crying/agitation — a key distinction that elevates this to moderate-severe croup requiring multiple interventions.
teaching_point: Stridor ONLY with crying/agitation = mild croup, potentially ESI 4. Stridor AT REST = moderate-severe croup, ESI 3. This distinction is critical for triage. Moderate-severe croup requires: (1) dexamethasone, (2) racemic epinephrine, (3) SpO2 monitoring, (4) ≥2h observation post-epi. That is definitionally a multi-resource visit. The AI's error here reflects pattern matching on 'croup' as a benign entity without integrating the rest severity modifier.

Sensation intact. Capillary refill brisk.
pmh: No prior ankle injuries. No bleeding disorders. Healthy, active child.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Negative Ottawa Ankle Rules in an 8-year-old (though Ottawa rules are less validated <18) with partial weight-bearing, lateral tenderness, and a mechanism consistent with lateral ligament sprain. One resource needed: X-ray to evaluate for growth plate fracture given skeletal immaturity. Can be safely triaged as ESI 4 with pain management and X-ray.
ai_rationale: AI correctly identifies partial weight-bearing ankle injury with negative Ottawa equivalent criteria as ESI 4 requiring X-ray only.
teaching_point: Ottawa Ankle Rules have reduced sensitivity in children due to growth plate vulnerability. Any pediatric ankle injury with bony tenderness should have imaging even with negative Ottawa criteria. However, this case is lateral ligament soft tissue injury — appropriate for ESI 4. Pain 4/10, partial weight-bearing, negative bony tenderness on exam = one resource (X-ray).

title: Cellulitis — Leg, Afebrile
hpi: 6-year-old male with 2-day history of increasing redness and swelling on his right lower leg. Mother noticed it after he scraped his knee at the playground 4 days ago. Area of erythema has expanded — she marked the border with a pen 8 hours ago and the redness has spread approximately 2cm beyond her mark. No fever at home. No drainage from wound. No other systemic symptoms.
exam: Alert, well-appearing, comfortable. 6x8cm area of erythema, warmth, and induration on anterior right lower leg. Non-fluctuant. No streaking (lymphangitis). Small dried abrasion at center of erythema. No lymphadenopathy. Wound margins well-defined. Afebrile in ED.
pmh: No prior skin infections. No diabetes or immunodeficiency. No prior MRSA. No recent antibiotics.
correct_esi_rationale: ESI 4: Afebrile, non-fluctuant, non-complicated cellulitis with clear trigger (abrasion) and well-demarcated borders. One resource: wound culture if clinical judgment dictates, plus prescription for antibiotics (cephalexin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for MRSA coverage if indicated). No IV needed, no imaging needed.
ai_rationale: AI flags the 2cm border progression in 8 hours as a high-risk feature and recommends ESI 3, citing concern for rapidly spreading cellulitis with possible bacteremia requiring IV access, CBC, and blood cultures. AI interprets spreading erythema as an early warning sign of sepsis in a pediatric patient.
teaching_point: AI overtriage: Spreading cellulitis does warrant monitoring, but this child is afebrile, hemodynamically normal, and well-appearing — classic ESI 4. ESI 3 would require a high-risk feature such as fever, systemic toxicity, concern for necrotizing fasciitis, or immunocompromise. Marking borders at triage is good practice, but 2cm spread over 8 hours in an afebrile child without systemic signs does not justify IV access or bloodwork. Teaching opportunity: recognize when AI pattern-matches to a dangerous diagnosis (spreading infection = sepsis) without integrating the full clinical picture.
